title: What Marketers Get Wrong About AI-Powered Analytics
body:
The marketing world has developed an almost religious enthusiasm for AI-powered analytics over the past two years. Platform vendors promise predictive insight at the push of a button; dashboards proliferate; and a new generation of tools claims to replace the analyst with an algorithm. Having worked at the intersection of marketing analytics and machine learning for over a decade, I have some complicated feelings about this.

The quality of the question matters more than the sophistication of the model

The most common mistake I see is treating AI as a source of questions rather than a tool for answering them. A model trained on historical data will tell you what predicted outcomes in the past — it won't tell you whether those patterns will hold in a market that has structurally shifted, or whether you're measuring the right outcome in the first place.

AI amplifies the quality of your thinking. If your inputs are shallow, your outputs will be confidently shallow.

Where AI genuinely helps

That said, the tools have become genuinely useful for specific analytical tasks: segmentation at scale, anomaly detection in large datasets, rapid iteration of demand forecasting models, and natural language interfaces for non-technical stakeholders to query data directly.

The wins come from deploying these capabilities against well-defined problems with good underlying data — not from hoping the AI will figure out what question to ask.
